title: Inset Bookshelf Doorway
summary: A working door built as a shallow bookshelf, hung in a matching frame — a hidden-passage bookcase that swings open on heavy hinges.
description:
This is the classic hidden-bookcase door: a shelf unit built into a door slab, hung in its own frame so it swings like a real door but reads as built-in shelving. It's the most advanced build in this collection because a door has to do two things at once — hold books and swing true for years — and a heavy, book-laden door punishes any sloppiness in the frame or hinges.

Everything is custom to your opening, so there's no fixed cut list; the numbers are all derived. Measure the opening, subtract 1/2" from height and width for a 1/4" gap all around, and work the frame from there. Because 2x4s are 3-1/2" wide, you subtract 7" from the door width to get the 2x6 cross-piece lengths. Build the frame, then dry-fit it in the opening and confirm the door swings freely with an even gap before you glue anything — this is why the source says explicitly not to glue the frame on the first assembly.

Weight and hinges are the whole ballgame. Use heavy-duty hinges rated well above the loaded weight, and for wide or heavily loaded doors add a caster wheel at the swinging bottom corner so the floor carries some of the load instead of the hinges alone. Pre-drill the pocket holes that attach the bookshelf to the door frame while the frame is apart — reaching them after assembly is miserable — then add trim or door-stop molding behind the frame to give the door a clean, quiet place to close.

tools.note: Frame joins with 1-1/2" pocket holes / 2-1/2" screws; the bookshelf with 3/4" holes / 1-1/4" screws.
A door has to hang plumb or it swings open or shut on its own.

materials.name: 1x6 board, 8 ft
materials.note: Shelves and sides — more for a wider or taller unit.

materials.name: Heavy-duty hinges
materials.note: Rated well above the loaded door weight — this is not the place to save money.

materials.name: Caster wheel
materials.note: At the swinging bottom corner for wide/heavy doors; lets the floor share the load.

materials.name: Door-stop molding
materials.note: Behind the frame, so the door has a clean place to close.

cutList.note: Cut to your opening minus 1/2" for a 1/4" gap all around. 79.5" shown for an 80" opening.
Door width minus 7" (two 2x4 stiles). Derived from your opening.
To fit inside the door frame.
As many as you want; length to the interior width.
Cut to the assembled shelf unit.

steps.title: Measure the opening and derive the door
steps.body: Measure the overall doorway opening, then subtract 1/2" from height and width so the finished door has a 1/4" clearance all around. From those numbers set the frame stile and rail lengths; remember 2x4s are 3-1/2" wide, so the 2x6 cross-pieces are the door width minus 7".

steps.title: Build and dry-fit the frame
steps.body: Assemble the door frame from 2x4s and 2x6s with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" screws — but do not glue yet. Fit the frame into the opening and confirm it swings and closes easily with an even gap. Adjust until it's right; gluing a frame that doesn't fit is a mistake you can't take back.

steps.title: Drill the bookshelf pockets, then reassemble
steps.body: Take the frame apart and drill 3/4"-stock pocket holes around all the inside edges where the bookshelf will attach — far easier now than reaching inside an assembled frame. Then reassemble the frame, this time with glue for a permanent joint.

steps.title: Build the bookshelf and back
steps.body: Build the shallow bookshelf from 1x6 sides and shelves to fit inside the frame, then glue-and-nail the 1/4" back on with finish nails — the back squares and stiffens it, which matters double on a door.

steps.title: Join shelf to frame and hang the door
steps.body: Attach the bookshelf to the frame through the predrilled 3/4" pocket holes with 1-1/4" screws and glue. Hang the door on heavy-duty hinges, adding a caster at the swinging bottom corner if it's wide or heavily loaded. Add door-stop molding behind the frame for a clean close, plus a lock or clasp. Load the shelves gradually and confirm it still swings true.

images.alt: Bookshelf built as a hidden door, swinging open in a doorway
